title: Studying the Significance of Surge Impedance and the Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) of a Transmission Line

content.content: To model a medium transmission line using a nominal Pi model in MATLAB/Simulink and to study the significance of Surge Impedance Loading (SIL). This experiment will help us analyze voltage and power profiles under the following loading conditions by varying the load resistance (ZL):

items: Loading at SIL (ZL = ZC)
Heavy Load / Over-Loaded (ZL < ZC)
Light Load / Under-Loaded (ZL > ZC)

content.content: **Simulink Components Used:**

items: Three-Phase Source
Three-Phase V-I Measurement
Pi Section Line
Three-Phase RLC Branch
Powergui
RMS blocks, Displays, and Scopes

content.content: **Line Parameters:**
Resistance (R) is varied during simulation to observe its impact on voltage and power due to the losses in the line.
A transmission line has series inductance (L) and shunt capacitance (C). For a lossless line, the surge impedance or characteristic impedance is given by:

The surge impedance is given by the ratio of voltage to current for a traveling wave on the line. When the load impedance equals the surge impedance, there are no reflections in the wave and the line operates under natural loading conditions.
The surge impedance loading (SIL) is the amount of power delivered to a purely resistive load when ZL = ZC, and it is given by:

During this loading condition, the reactive power generated by the shunt capacitance is equal to the reactive power absorbed by the series inductance. This balance keeps the voltage constant along the line.
**Effect of Loading on Line Behavior:**

items: **ZL > ZC (Load < SIL):** The line behaves capacitively and supplies reactive power. Receiving end voltage will rise (VR > VS). Controlled using shunt reactors.
**ZL = ZC (Load = SIL):** Reactive power is balanced and voltage profile remains constant (VR ≈ VS).
**ZL < ZC (Load > SIL):** The line behaves inductively and absorbs reactive power. Receiving end voltage will fall (VR < VS). Controlled using shunt capacitors.

content.content: SIL serves as a benchmark for voltage stability and efficient power transfer in transmission systems.
**Core Simulink Model — Nominal Pi Transmission Line**

**Complete Simulink Setup**

content.caption: Figure 2: Complete Simulink setup of the medium transmission line model including measurement and display blocks

content.content: The above figure shows the complete Simulink model diagram of the medium transmission line using the nominal Pi configuration. In this setup, voltage and current goto blocks are connected at both sending and receiving ends along with scopes and display units to observe output voltages, current waveforms, and real and reactive power flow under various loading conditions.

items: Open MATLAB/Simulink and create a new model.
Build the nominal Pi transmission line model using the Pi Section Line block with the given parameters.
Connect a Three-Phase Source at the sending end with source voltage 7 kV (RMS, line-to-line) at 50 Hz.
Connect Three-Phase V-I Measurement blocks at both sending and receiving ends.
Connect Power Measurement (Three-Phase) blocks to measure active and reactive power at both ends.
Connect RMS blocks to measure RMS values of voltages and currents.
Add Scopes and Display blocks to observe the output waveforms and numerical values.
Set the load resistance ZL = ZC = 500 Ω (SIL condition) and run the simulation. Record VS, VR, PS, PR, QS, QR.
Change ZL = 100 Ω (heavy load, ZL < ZC) and run the simulation. Record the readings.
Change ZL = 1000 Ω (light load, ZL > ZC) and run the simulation. Record the readings.
Compare the voltage and power profiles across all three loading conditions and analyze the results.

content.content: MATLAB/Simulink was used to simulate the medium transmission line under three loading conditions: loading at SIL (ZL = 500 Ω), heavy load (ZL = 100 Ω), and light load (ZL = 1000 Ω). The nominal Pi model was used with the parameters listed in the apparatus section.

The order of waveforms is the same for all loading conditions: sending end voltage, then receiving end voltage, followed by sending end current and receiving end current at the bottom. For power waveforms: sending end active power at the top, then receiving end active power, followed by sending end reactive power and receiving end reactive power at the bottom.

**Solved Problems**

**Q1.** A three-phase, 50 Hz transmission line has a per-kilometer inductance of 1.2 mH/km and a capacitance of 0.009 µF/km. The line operates at a line-to-line voltage of 220 kV. Determine: (1) The surge impedance of the line. (2) The surge impedance loading (SIL) in megawatts (MW).
**Solution:** Per-kilometer values given: L′ = 1.2 × 10⁻³ H/km, C′ = 0.009 × 10⁻⁶ F/km = 9 × 10⁻⁹ F/km, VLL = 220 × 10³ V.
Surge impedance:
Surge Impedance Loading (SIL):

**Q2.** A three-phase, 50 Hz transmission line has a per-kilometer inductance of 4.2 mH/km and a capacitance of 0.027 µF/km. The line operates at a line-to-line voltage of 440 kV. Determine: (1) The surge impedance of the line. (2) The surge impedance loading (SIL) in megawatts (MW).
**Solution:** Per-kilometer values given: L′ = 4.2 × 10⁻³ H/km, C′ = 0.027 × 10⁻⁶ F/km = 2.7 × 10⁻⁸ F/km, VLL = 440 × 10³ V.
Surge impedance:
Surge Impedance Loading (SIL):

From the theoretical calculations, the surge impedance of the line was found to be ZC = 500 Ω and the corresponding Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) was approximately 0.098 MW for a line-to-line voltage of 7 kV. In the simulation, when ZL = ZC, the measured power is 0.0976 MW, which is approximately equal to the theoretical value, indicating the correctness of the model.

items: **Case 1 (ZL = ZC):** The line operates at its natural loading. The sending end voltage VS and the receiving end voltage VR are equal. Both active powers PS and PR are also equal and reactive power is negligible, indicating a balanced reactive condition.

**Case 2 (ZL < ZC):** This represents a heavy loading or overload condition. The line shows inductive behavior — VR drops below VS, and the system absorbs reactive power. The current and active power values increase compared to SIL loading.

**Case 3 (ZL > ZC):** This is a lightly loaded or underload condition. The line shows capacitive behavior — VR becomes greater than VS. The line supplies reactive power to the system and the active power decreases.

content.content: The experiment confirms that at SIL, voltage remains almost flat across the line; under overloading, voltage drops and the line absorbs reactive power; and under underloading, voltage rises and the line generates reactive power.
**Q1. What is surge impedance and how is it different from load impedance?**

**A:** Surge impedance (or characteristic impedance) ZC is an intrinsic property of a transmission line defined as ZC = \sqrt{L/C}, where L and C are the per-unit-length series inductance and shunt capacitance of the line. It represents the impedance seen by a traveling wave on a lossless line and is independent of line length or termination. Load impedance ZL, on the other hand, is the impedance of the actual load connected at the receiving end and can take any value depending on the load. When ZL = ZC, the line operates at its natural loading (SIL) with no reflections and a flat voltage profile.
**Q2. What is Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) and why is it used as a reference for transmission line performance?**

**A:** SIL is the real power delivered by a transmission line to a purely resistive load equal to ZC when the line is energized at rated voltage. It is given by SIL = V²/ZC. At SIL, the reactive power generated by the line's shunt capacitance exactly equals the reactive power absorbed by the series inductance, resulting in a flat voltage profile (VR ≈ VS) and zero net reactive power exchange with the system. SIL is used as a reference because it represents the most efficient natural operating condition of the line, where no external reactive compensation is needed.
**Q3. Why does the receiving end voltage rise above the sending end voltage under light loading conditions?**

**A:** Under light loading (ZL > ZC), the load current drawn is small, so the series voltage drop across the line inductance is small. However, the shunt capacitance of the line still generates reactive power proportional to the line voltage, and this reactive power is not fully absorbed by the small inductive current. The excess reactive power causes the voltage to build up along the line, resulting in the receiving end voltage exceeding the sending end voltage. This phenomenon is known as the Ferranti effect and is more pronounced on lightly loaded or no-load long lines.
**Q4. What is the nominal Pi model and why is it used for medium transmission lines?**

**A:** The nominal Pi model represents a medium transmission line (typically 80–250 km) by placing the total series impedance (R + jωL) as a single lumped element in the middle, and splitting the total shunt admittance (jωC) equally into two halves placed at the sending and receiving ends, forming a π-shaped circuit. It is used for medium lines because it provides a reasonable approximation of the distributed nature of line parameters without requiring the full distributed parameter model. Short lines (< 80 km) neglect shunt capacitance entirely, while long lines (> 250 km) require exact distributed parameter or hyperbolic models.
**Q5. What remedial measures are used to control overvoltage and undervoltage conditions on a transmission line?**

**A:** For light loading conditions (ZL > ZC) where VR > VS (overvoltage), shunt reactors are connected at the receiving end or along the line to absorb the excess reactive power generated by the line capacitance, thereby reducing the voltage. For heavy loading conditions (ZL < ZC) where VR < VS (undervoltage), shunt capacitor banks or Static VAR Compensators (SVCs) are used to supply the reactive power demanded by the inductive loading, supporting the receiving end voltage. Series capacitors are also used to reduce the effective series reactance and improve voltage regulation under heavy loading.
**Q6. How does the simulation result for the SIL case validate the theoretical calculation?**

**A:** The theoretical SIL was calculated as SIL = V²/ZC = (7000)²/500 = 98 kW = 0.098 MW. In the simulation with ZL = ZC = 500 Ω, the measured active power at the sending end was 0.0976 MW, which is within 0.25% of the theoretical value. Additionally, the simulation shows VS = VR = 4.0320 kV (flat voltage profile) and QS = QR ≈ 0 (balanced reactive power), both of which are characteristic signatures of SIL operation. The close agreement between theoretical and simulated values validates the correctness of the Simulink model and the nominal Pi approximation for this line length.

From the theoretical calculations, the surge impedance of the line was found to be ZC = 500 Ω and the corresponding Surge Impedance Loading (SIL) was approximately 0.098 MW for a line-to-line voltage of 7 kV. The simulation results for all three loading conditions — loading at SIL, heavy loading, and light loading — closely matched the theoretical predictions, validating the nominal Pi model.

The variation in voltage, current, and reactive power under different loading conditions clearly demonstrates the effect of Surge Impedance Loading on the performance of a medium transmission line. At SIL, voltage remains almost flat across the line; under overloading, voltage drops and the line absorbs reactive power; and under underloading, voltage rises and the line generates reactive power. Overall, the experiment confirms SIL as a fundamental benchmark for voltage stability and efficient power transfer in transmission systems.
